It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. You may also `predict(expect_change=..., expected_grid=...)` before an `action(...)` to test your world model; `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats` then score it, and a `surprise` flags where your model was wrong -- optional, use it when you hold a belief worth checking.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 68 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 2.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.content:
Clicking on the right side of the face (row 30, col 45) didn't move the blue squares. They're still at:
- Left: rows 54-58, cols 14-18
- Right: rows 54-58, cols 44-48
I've been spending too many actions on exploring. Let me step back and think about the goal more carefully.
I know:
1. UP/DOWN moves the blue squares vertically (left moves 3 rows per action, right moves 2 rows per action).
2. MOUSE on the left eye socket (row 16, col 21) moved the blue squares apart horizontally.
3. Other MOUSE clicks don't seem to move the blue squares.
4. SPACE doesn't complete the level.
5. LEFT/RIGHT don't move the blue squares.
Let me think about what the goal could be. The face has two blue squares at the bottom. The blue squares can move up, down, and horizontally. The goal might be to move the blue squares to specific positions.
Looking at the face, the blue squares are at the bottom of the black shape. The black shape has "eye socket" regions at rows 14-18. Maybe the goal is to move the blue squares to the eye socket positions.
But the blue squares can only reach rows 39-43 (left) and rows 44-48 (right) when they move up. They can't reach rows 14-18 using UP/DOWN actions.
Wait, maybe the goal is to make the blue squares align at the same row. Currently, they're at rows 54-58 (both). So they're already at the same row.
Maybe the goal is to make the blue squares align at the same column. Currently, they're at cols 14-18 (left) and cols 44-48 (right). So they're at different columns.
Maybe the goal is to move the blue squares to the same column. But the blue squares can only move horizontally when I press MOUSE on certain regions. And clicking on the left eye socket caused the blue squares to move apart horizontally, not towards each other.
Let me try a different approach. Maybe the goal is to move the blue squares to the eye socket positions using a combination of UP/DOWN and MOUSE actions.
The eye sockets are at rows 14-18. The blue squares can only reach rows 39-43 (left) and rows 44-48 (right) when they move up. So the blue squares can't reach rows 14-18 using UP/DOWN actions.
Wait, maybe I need to look at the face more carefully. The face has two "eye socket" regions at rows 14-18. The eye sockets are empty (no blue squares). Maybe the goal is to fill the eye sockets with blue squares.
But the blue squares can only reach rows 39-43 (left) and rows 44-48 (right) when they move up. So the blue squares can't reach rows 14-18 using UP/DOWN actions.
Hmm, maybe the goal is not to move the blue squares to the eye socket positions. Maybe the goal is to move the blue squares to specific positions where they form a "smile" or "frown" on the face.
Actually, let me look at the image more carefully. The blue squares are at the bottom of the black shape. The black shape has a "mouth" region at the bottom. The blue squares are at the bottom of the mouth region.
Maybe the goal is to move the blue squares to the "eye socket" positions. But the blue squares can't reach rows 14-18 using UP/DOWN actions.
Wait, maybe I need to use MOUSE to click on the eye socket positions to move the blue squares there. Let me try clicking on the left eye socket position (row 16, col 19) to see if the left blue square moves there.
Actually, I already tried clicking on the left eye socket (row 16, col 21), and it moved the blue squares horizontally, not vertically.
Let me try a completely different approach. Maybe the goal is to make the blue squares disappear or change color. Let me try pressing SPACE multiple times to see if something happens.
Actually, I've pressed SPACE multiple times, and it just counts as a meaningful action. It doesn't complete the level.
Let me think about this differently. The face has two blue squares at the bottom. The blue squares can move up, down, and horizontally. The goal might be to move the blue squares to specific positions to form a "face" with eyes and a mouth.
Looking at the face, the blue squares are at the bottom of the black shape. The black shape has "eye socket" regions at rows 14-18. Maybe the goal is to move the blue squares to the eye socket positions.
But the blue squares can only reach rows 39-43 (left) and rows 44-48 (right) when they move up. They can't reach rows 14-18 using UP/DOWN actions.
Wait, maybe I need to look at the initial state of the game to see what the blue squares looked like at the start. The initial state had the blue squares at rows 54-58, cols 19-23 (left) and rows 54-58, cols 39-43 (right).
Maybe the goal is to return the blue squares to their initial positions. Currently, the blue squares are at rows 54-58, cols 14-18 (left) and rows 54-58, cols 44-48 (right). So the blue squares are at the same row as the initial state, but at different columns.
To return the blue squares to their initial positions, I need to move them horizontally. The left blue square needs to move from cols 14-18 to cols 19-23 (5 columns right), and the right blue square needs to move from cols 44-48 to cols 39-43 (5 columns left).
But I don't know how to move the blue squares horizontally towards each other. Clicking on the left eye socket caused the blue squares to move apart horizontally. Maybe clicking on the right eye socket causes the blue squares to move towards each other.
Let me try clicking on the right eye socket again, but this time at a different position. The right eye socket is approximately at rows 14-18, cols 39-43. Let me try clicking on row 16, col 39.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state
nodes = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for node in nodes:
    if node['color'] == 'S':
        b = node['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Blue square id={node['id']} bbox=({min(rows)},{min(cols)})-({max(rows)},{max(cols)})")

# Let me try clicking on the right eye socket at a different position
# The right eye socket is approximately at rows 14-18, cols 39-43
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 16, 'col': 39}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Blue square id=5 bbox=(54,14)-(58,18)
Blue square id=6 bbox=(54,44)-(58,48)
